stories.headline: Grand jury to investigate fatal Aurora police shooting of Rajon Belt-Stubblefield
stories.summary:
An Arapahoe County grand jury will determine whether Aurora Police Officer Matthew Neely should face criminal charges in the fatal shooting of Rajon Belt-Stubblefield, 37, a Black man killed on Aug. 30 following a traffic stop attempt near East Sixth Avenue and Sable Boulevard. The 18th Judicial District's Critical Incident Response Team completed an investigation, which the district attorney's office reviewed before referring the case to a grand jury for further independent examination.

Aurora police said Belt-Stubblefield crashed into two vehicles while evading the stop, then tossed a gun into the grass and continued walking toward Neely despite orders to stop. Police Chief Todd Chamberlain blamed Belt-Stubblefield for the confrontation, but the man's family contends body camera footage shows Neely first grabbed Belt-Stubblefield by the back of the neck before the situation escalated. The family has announced plans to sue the city.

The grand jury will review evidence, conduct additional investigation as it deems appropriate, and determine whether probable cause exists for formal criminal charges. The referral comes amid heightened scrutiny of the Aurora Police Department, which faces court-ordered reforms and recently drew criticism from an independent monitor over three separate fatal shootings of people experiencing mental health crises.

stories.headline: Rockies top prospect Ethan Holliday to miss remainder of 2026 season with stress fracture
stories.summary:
Colorado Rockies top prospect Ethan Holliday will undergo surgery for a stress fracture in his left foot and miss the rest of the 2026 season, MLB.com reported Friday. The 19-year-old shortstop, selected fourth overall in the 2025 MLB Draft, has not played for Low-A Fresno since May 20 and was placed on the 7-day injured list on May 24.

Before the injury, Holliday was enjoying a breakout campaign, slashing .262/.395/.557 with nine home runs and 32 RBIs in 33 games. He is ranked the 17th overall prospect by MLB Pipeline and is the son of former Rockies star Matt Holliday and younger brother of Baltimore Orioles second baseman Jackson Holliday.

The injury is a significant blow to the organization's rebuilding plans, though other prospects continue to develop. Triple-A Albuquerque's Charlie Condon is riding a 10-game hitting streak, while Low-A Fresno's Roldy Brito is slashing .338/.383/.527 with 45 RBIs across 45 games, with a promotion appearing imminent. The major league club sits at 21-37, the worst record in baseball, having lost eight of its last ten games.

stories.headline: Broncos retain assistant GM Reed Burckhardt after Vikings choose Seattle's Nolan Teasley
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos assistant general manager Reed Burckhardt will remain in his role after the Minnesota Vikings passed over him to hire Seattle Seahawks assistant general manager Nolan Teasley as their new GM. Burckhardt had completed two rounds of interviews with Minnesota, including one remote session and one in-person meeting, for the position that had been vacant since January.

Burckhardt spent 13 seasons with the Vikings before joining Denver in 2022 as director of player personnel, working under Broncos GM George Paton for 12 of those years when Paton was Minnesota's assistant GM. Paton brought Burckhardt to Denver a year after his own hiring in 2021, and promoted him to assistant general manager last year.

Other finalists for the Vikings job included Minnesota interim GM Rob Brzezinski, who will remain with the organization, Buffalo's Terrance Gray and the Los Angeles Rams' John McKay. Burckhardt's retention provides continuity in Denver's front office as the Broncos continue building under Paton's direction.

stories.headline: Aurora's 1,600-home Prairie Point development breaks ground along E-470 corridor
stories.summary:
Oakwood Homes broke ground Thursday on Prairie Point, a master-planned community spanning more than 900 acres along the E-470 corridor near Parker Road in Aurora that will deliver more than 1,600 homes when fully built. City officials, including Councilwoman Francoise Bergan and Mayor Mike Coffman, attended the ceremony and described the project as a meaningful step toward addressing the metro area's persistent housing shortage.

The development will offer a mix of housing types at a range of price points, though Coffman said units will be geared more toward middle-income and above buyers. Colorado Association of Home Builders CEO Ted Leighty said master-planned communities like Prairie Point are increasingly critical given resident opposition to higher-density infill development in established neighborhoods. The median price of a residential property sold in Denver metro in April was $605,000, up from $590,000 in March.

Denver ranked 13th highest in median home prices among 49 metro areas analyzed by Redfin, with the national median at $396,173. A recent National Association of Realtors report found that while overall inventory has increased, a significant share of buyers cannot afford available homes, with the affordability score for the nation at 74.9 percent — still below the pre-pandemic level of 84.4 percent.

stories.headline: Denver Downtown Development Authority to invest $3 million in Denver Pavilions upgrades
stories.summary:
The Denver Downtown Development Authority announced Friday that it will invest approximately $3 million in capital improvements at Denver Pavilions, targeting the parking garage, roof, exterior signage and a new public mural along 15th Street. The largest share — about $2.4 million — will fund parking garage upgrades including replacement of the carbon dioxide system, repairs to degraded areas, and a new gate and payment system. Work is expected to begin by July and continue for roughly 18 months.

Roof maintenance and repairs totaling approximately $350,000 are scheduled to begin in August, while a $300,000 investment will fund a new mural on the south-facing facade of the building. Local artist Olive Moya has been selected to design the new work, which will replace an existing Clyfford Still mural and is expected to be installed this summer. The DDDA also confirmed that the escalator to the Regal Cinema has been repaired, with a second escalator near Lucky Strike set to follow in June.

The improvements come after the DDDA acquired the Pavilions and two adjacent surface parking lots late last year, retaining an additional $8 million in funds for property improvements. The Pavilions is also expanding programming, including a touring human anatomy exhibition that made its U.S. debut on May 17 and is scheduled through June 28, with a possible extension through September due to strong early ticket sales.

stories.headline: Severe thunderstorm watch issued for Colorado Eastern Plains through Saturday evening
stories.summary:
The National Weather Service issued a severe thunderstorm watch Saturday for seven Eastern Plains counties — Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Sedgwick, Washington, Weld and Yuma — through 9 p.m., warning of potential tornadoes, tennis-ball-sized hail and wind gusts up to 70 mph. Forecasters said communities could face vehicle damage from hail and structural damage to roofs, siding and trees from high winds.

The watch covers portions of Colorado, Nebraska, South Dakota and Wyoming as part of a broader storm system. The Denver metro area itself is expected to see clearing conditions Friday night following Thursday's storms, with a mainly dry and pleasant weekend ahead. Daytime highs are forecast in the upper 70s Saturday and low 80s Sunday.

The unsettled weather pattern is expected to continue into early next week, with scattered afternoon storms possible Monday and Tuesday before a drying trend begins mid-week. Forecasters are tracking a potential summer-like heat wave for the Denver metro late next week, with highs possibly reaching the upper 80s and the city's first 90-degree day of the year possible.

stories.headline: Community nonprofit races to raise $1.5 million to keep historic Jamestown Mercantile in local hands
stories.summary:
A newly formed nonprofit called The Jamestown Home for Wayward Artists, Pirates and the Somewhat Feral is working to purchase the building housing the Jamestown Mercantile Cafe, the only restaurant and bar in the small mountain community about 14 miles north of Boulder. Owner Rainbow Shultz and four friends established the 501(c)(3) after learning the roughly 130-year-old building could go to an outside buyer, and secured an 11-month letter of intent with building owner Loupee Burton Jr. granting exclusive purchase rights at $1.5 million.

As of late May, the group had raised approximately $33,000 from 130 donors, representing about 2.2 percent of the asking price. The fundraising effort received a notable boost when Colorado musician Gregory Alan Isakov committed to donate $1 from every ticket sold at his summer performances, a figure that could reach between $3,000 and $20,000. Isakov has deep ties to the Mercantile, having performed at open-mic nights there in the early 2000s while studying at Naropa University and still supplying the venue with vegetables from his Starling Farm east of Boulder.

The building houses the cafe and bar, a music venue that turns away roughly half of the bands requesting to play there, four apartments and space for local artists in two adjacent structures. Supporters say the Mercantile serves as a cultural anchor for Jamestown and surrounding communities, and losing it to outside development interests would fundamentally change the character of the town.

stories.headline: Denver residents weigh in on bus rapid transit plans for Colorado, Speer and Federal boulevards
stories.summary:
Denver Post readers offered sharp and divided opinions on proposed bus rapid transit projects along several major Denver corridors, including Colorado Boulevard, Speer Boulevard, Federal Boulevard and Leetsdale Drive. The letters were prompted by a May 27 news story on the Colorado Boulevard BRT proposal and came days after the city of Glendale formally pushed back against the plan, citing concerns about traffic congestion and impacts to businesses along the corridor.

Several writers expressed strong opposition, arguing that converting travel lanes for bus-only use would worsen congestion, harm local businesses and drive residents to shop in surrounding suburbs such as Centennial, Littleton, Englewood and Aurora. Critics also questioned the democratic legitimacy of the projects, arguing that residents had not voted on the transportation changes.

At least one letter writer offered a more favorable view based on personal experience with Boulder's bus-lane conversion on Arapahoe Avenue, describing improved traffic flow while noting some minor issues with lane markings. The letters reflect broader tensions in Denver over the Johnston administration's transportation priorities as the city continues to pursue a network of bus rapid transit routes on major arterial streets.

stories.headline: Maroon Bells management may shift to Pitkin County as Forest Service faces funding and staffing shortfalls
stories.summary:
The U.S. Forest Service is in discussions that could transfer day-to-day management of the Maroon Bells — one of Colorado's most visited and photographed natural landmarks — to Pitkin County as federal staffing and funding pressures mount. The site near Aspen draws hundreds of thousands of visitors annually and has required reservation systems and shuttle operations to manage crowds.

Details of a potential management agreement, including funding arrangements and operational responsibilities, are still being worked out between the Forest Service and county officials. The discussions reflect broader strains on federal land management agencies in Colorado, which have faced budget constraints and staffing reductions that have prompted other alternative management arrangements across the state.

Pitkin County officials have not publicly opposed taking on a larger role, but the shift would represent a significant change in how one of the state's most iconic recreation destinations is operated. Any formal agreement would need to address issues including fee collection, law enforcement, emergency services and environmental stewardship at the heavily trafficked wilderness gateway.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis appears at Democratic Party event with tape over his mouth, one week after party censure
stories.summary:
Colorado Gov. Jared Polis appeared on a Democratic Party Zoom call with tape over his mouth, a pointed visual gesture one week after the state Democratic Party formally censured him. The censure, which was a rebuke from within his own party, has drawn sustained attention to tensions between Polis and progressive factions of Colorado Democrats.

The governor did not publicly explain the tape gesture during or after the call, but the imagery was widely interpreted as a response to the censure and an assertion that party leadership was attempting to silence him. Polis has increasingly positioned himself as an independent-minded Democrat willing to diverge from progressive orthodoxy on issues including fiscal policy and business regulation.

The episode adds another chapter to an ongoing intraparty conflict that has been playing out as Colorado Democrats navigate a competitive 2026 election cycle, including a contested U.S. Senate primary and a wide-open gubernatorial race. The censure and its aftermath have drawn national attention to divisions within the Colorado Democratic Party.

stories.headline: State Sen. Julie Gonzales challenges Hickenlooper in U.S. Senate Democratic primary
stories.summary:
Colorado state Sen. Julie Gonzales has formally entered the Democratic primary race for U.S. Senate, mounting a challenge to former Gov. and Sen. John Hickenlooper. Gonzales, a progressive from Denver, framed her campaign in part as a rebuke of what she called "do-nothing" Democrats in the U.S. Senate, signaling a clear ideological contrast with the more centrist Hickenlooper.

Gonzales has served in the state Senate representing a Denver district and has been a prominent voice on immigration, criminal justice reform and housing policy at the state level. Her entry into the race sets up a competitive primary that will test the relative strength of progressive versus moderate factions within the Colorado Democratic Party heading into the June 30 primary.

The Senate seat is considered a key race in the broader national battle for Senate control. Hickenlooper, who won the seat in 2020, has not yet formally announced his re-election bid but is widely expected to seek another term. The primary contest adds to an already turbulent period for Colorado Democrats, who are also managing a contested gubernatorial primary and ongoing fallout from the recent censure of Gov. Polis.

stories.headline: Trial opens for Jefferson County man accused of shooting teenager who knocked on barn door
stories.summary:
A trial began this week in Jefferson County for a man accused of shooting a teenager in the face after the boy and other youths knocked on the door of his barn. The case drew significant regional attention when it was first reported, raising questions about the use of deadly force in response to what the defense has characterized as a startling nighttime intrusion on private property.

Prosecutors contend the shooting was unjustified, while defense attorneys are expected to argue that their client reasonably feared for his safety when confronted with unexpected visitors at his property. The victim survived the shooting but sustained serious injuries.

Jefferson County courts have seen several high-profile cases in recent years involving disputes over property, firearms and self-defense claims. The trial is expected to last several days, with jury selection and opening arguments getting underway this week.

weather.notable: About 11 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: Thirteen-year-olds are the meanest people in the world. They terrify me to this day.
